item.title: My Etch A Sketch art is in the Netflix film Spiderhead!!!
item.description:
I've got 6 Etch A Sketch drawings in the Netflix movie Spiderhead! I created these drawings in November/December 2020 and have been waiting to share this news ever since.
It's surreal to see my name in movie credits. This isn't exactly the kind of film I would expect to see an Etch A Sketch in, but I am absolutely honored to pursue all-new frontier in my ridiculously niche choice of medium. It's incredible to realize that (as far as my research has shown), I am the very first Etch A Sketch artist to have their art in a bona fide movie!
item.dc:creator: pikajane

item.title: "One of Those Days" cartoons
item.description: Lulu Tax!! She has 2 Xtra toe beans on each foot. We named her after the anime we were watching at the time. Code Geass
item.dc:creator: cowboywest07

item.title: Officially a Dr!!
item.description: Hi Imgur sorry for the selfie video, but couldn’t resist! I have successfully defended my PhD and I can finally call myself a DR of Psychology! Words cannot express how happy and relieved I am, it’s been a long journey and with COVID I never thought it would end. Huge thank you to the Imgur community for keeping me up at night and getting me through revisions, corrections and my defense. You are all the best ? P.S: special thanks to my amazing husband who introduced me to this community and got me through my PhD in one piece.
item.dc:creator: elenskiwang

item.title: At least they’re getting better tax benefits now?
item.dc:creator: MrBobSaget

item.title: I did a thing?
item.description: Was a little uneasy with a message in the morning asking “Do you remember me?”. Of course I remembered this kid. I hoped he wouldn’t hurt himself or someone else. Wore some Nazi paraphernalia and was just angry. I didn’t think any of my conversations made a difference but maybe they had? He messaged me to tell me he was sorry for how he acted and that he threw away his Nazi pins in the trash. Told me he was doing okay and he wanted to send me recordings of his band. I’m just happy he realized, fuck nazi punks and that he is doing alright. To the teachers out there struggling. I guess we really can make a difference.
item.dc:creator: ilikepiewaytoomuch

item.title: Time to do the thing
item.description:
We met on the 4th of July. Been engaged since the beginning of COVID and have tried planning the wedding multiple times only to have the pandemic cancel our plans. Last week we just said to hell with all the plans and decided to have a small ceremony at the bar I work at during the summers. I’m psyched. I’m so ready to call this magnificent woman my wife.
Sorry for the selfie. We’re too cute to not post one though.
item.dc:creator: illhaveaguinness

item.title: Donate to competitive races. Help prevent this!
item.description: I'm a healthcare provider, and this terrifies me. We need to mobilize now! Competitive swing races need money to get their message out and whip up voters. Warnock, for instance. Or Kelley, Demmings or Fetterman. Avoid PACs as you don't know where the money will go for sure. We have to maintain majorities +2 senate seats. And we can. And we MUST!

item.title: $20k Cabin
item.description:
I built this little lake cabin entirely by myself (only hired exterior painter), using a lot of second-use materials (windows, flooring, rigid insulation boards, cedar fence pickets). It's been a really fun build! I even did the design and renders myself in Google SketchUp. Easy to learn. Total cost so far is around $18k, including lumber to finish the back porch, add stairs & railings, and trim for the interior. I expect the final build to be just under $20k.
Floor plan for base level. Loft is over the bedroom/closet area. 320sqft inside.
Framing render
Original render I made in SketchUp before buying any materials, or really knowing anything about building the cabin
Came out pretty close to the render - Cedar tree porch supports harvested from the lot
Back view, metal roof
Side view - mini split
Construction zone. Don't judge the janky ceiling joints. It's tough hanging 8x12 sheets of 5/8 drywall solo, on a sloped ceiling! I'll be covering the joints with battens. Loft above the bedroom.
Ceiling drywall finished (joints still ugly). Going to add short wall for loft.
Living room area
Basic kitchen - live edge countertop, test fitting the sink
Sealing the butcher block counter, love the look
Barn doors to save room & b/c trendy AF
Couch (folds into bed) & tools
LVP flooring going in the bedroom
Bathroom - tub/shower behind the curtain
Lake view of the cove, approximately 200ft from the front porch
My running list of materials/costs
item.dc:creator: LoopholeTravel

item.title: Please. Please. Please. Please. Please let there be hope.
item.description: https://www.sltrib.com/news/2022/06/27/utahs-planned-parenthood/ I am one of the 55 women that was scheduled to "take a pill" at the SLC planned parenthood location this week, and I would REALLY like to not have to drive to Colorado just to take it. Background: married 9 years, never been pregnant before, actively taking birth control but I missed my period last weekend and discovered I was pregnant on Monday. This entire fiasco has been something I never imagined being in and I cry myself to sleep on a nightly basis. Please let there be hope. Edit: Holy shit, guys! WE WON! We actually won! At least temporarily... https://www.sltrib.com/news/2022/06/27/utahs-planned-parenthood/
item.dc:creator: sailormars69420

item.title: Presenting: A story
item.description: I think I look hot in a sun dress. There's really nothing more to it than that. This didn't start out as an expercise in trying to learn anything or grow. It was really just that. “I think I look hot in a sundress.” It had been a really great week at work (I work for a large tech company doing tech company things, they're relatively progressive but still not progressive enough that I feel comfortable naming them here) but I had a great week. This Friday I didn't have a lot to do for work, I had tickets to go to an outdoor Folk Music Festival in downtown Durham North Carolina. It was sunny. It was a nice 80 degrees with a breeze and I thought “I would look good in a sundress” and quite frankly, I always thought it was kind of rude that women had a monopoly on sundresses. They're so comfortable! I don't think it's fair that men can't wear them, but neither here nor there, that's how it all started. So I put on a sundress and that is the beginning of what became a an eye-opening spiritual and emotional adventure for me. So to start out, I guess, we'll begin with how most would describe me or how most would judge me upon meeting me for the first time. I'm a six foot one, white male with green eyes, light brown hair. I have a good upper middle-class job. I'm a Christ follower. I look exactly like what you're probably picturing the prototypical white male to look like. I am the poster boy for White Privilege, at least, if you just look at me on the outside. In reality, when I say that I'm a Christ follower I belive in loving God and Loving People. I'm bisexual. I'm a father of two amazing young boys. I'm an Iraq war veteran. I've been a firefighter and EMT. I've climbed to see the top and I have fallen into the deepest depths. But that's the complicated man. That's the me that really only I know and that even that only scratches the surface. With that being said, I didn't think anything of putting on a sundress. I really didn't. It seems like it would be comfortable. I thought I looked good in it. Why not? So, I got into an Uber and I came in contact with my first stranger. I really wasn't sure how this would go. I was about to judge them the way we all have a tendency to do. He was a middle eastern gentleman. He was Muslim by faith himself (I could see some of the literature that he had in the front seat). But there was not the reaction that I was expecting, and this would be a recurring theme throughout the day. See, I guess a little of my aprehension was anticipating, if not hostility, at least discomfort with how I was dressed and how I would be perceived. Unbeknownst to me at the time I didn't realize that for most people when you see how I was presenting, which would be a male but wearing typically female presenting clothing in the form of a sundress, it could be most accurately assumed that I would be a member of the transgender community. (Let me be very clear: It was in NO WAY my intention to go out in “Trans Face.” Fact is I DO look good in a sundress and I'll do it again. They are comfortable!) I didn't think about this before, but the reaction of my Uber driver wasn't one of hostility or of apprehension. I could actually feel that they were more comfortable with me because I was not dressed in typical cis, White, Hetero Male garb. I wasn't being responded to the way that I was used to because I didn't realize there was another way to be responded to. The way that he responded to me with almost born-in kinship. Almost, “You're like me. People treat you differently because of how you look, and we have that in common and I'm relieved that it's you getting in my car and not what could just as easily have been (especially in today's day and age) a white supremacist. Or any other number of intolerant people that live in the South and all over the country that look just like me. That was unexpected. I've never felt that before. And it wouldn't be the first time it would be a feature of this, what turned out to be, an amazing spiritual experience. It was the tip of the iceberg. So, as we arrived Downtown I got out of the Uber, and I was at the Durham Bulls Stadium where they were having this Music festival. But as soon as I step out onto the streets, I could feel something change. And I can't tell you what it was, and it was interesting because I am again, a six foot one Caucasian male. I'm in pretty decent shape. I'm typically not used to being regarded, and if I am, I'm used to being regarded in a certain way. But I could tell that that was all gone now. I was coming into contact now with those who would under most circumstances be a part of my “Group”. For lack of a better term, White People. Now I started to get the looks. I started to feel what I can only imagine our brothers and cisters in the transgender Community feel every day. Six foot one, White, Male, Iraq War veteran. Scared. I was scared. The Situation around me had turned not overtly hostile but there was an atmosphere of hostility under the surface of every glance. At first it felt like this may have been just my perception. But it was real. It didn't take long as I was walking off towards the American Tobacco campus where I was going to grab lunch that, being new to sundresses, I made an error. I did not think to keep my hands down when the winds blew to keep my skirt from blowing up. I know ,I'm new to this. So my skirt blew up. I was a little embarrassed, but again, I'm a pretty confident guy. I just kind of laughed it off, but then I heard my first quips. And it came from a group of white men. Probably late 20s early 30s. The jokes started coming. They looked just like me. “Well, would you look at that? She's hung just like a dude!” “That's a big dick for a lady!” “They think they're passing!?” I wanted to say something, but nothing really came to mind to be completely honest. Since I became a Christ follower and finding my peace and my Sanctuary within Christ's Church I don't feel the need to engage in aggressive or hostile actions like I used to. So I just let that soak-in because it wasn't true. I wasn't trying to pass. I do have a dick. And if I was a lady, I would assume I'd be pretty endowed for one. But it still hurt even though it wasn't true. What if it was? What if all I wanted in the world was to feel and look on the outside how I felt on the inside? What of the hurt then? I got that feeling. That feeling you get when you're about to get into a fight or you feel like you're about to get into a fight. Where your muscles become a little bit lighter and your heart rate goes up a little bit more. From that moment on and for the remainder of the night, that never went away. That feeling of high alert. Listening out. But that wasn't the feeling or the reaction I got from everyone. In fact quite the oposite. Love and kindness in this world is very much alive. The Waitresses that served me at the restaurant could not have been sweeter. The women that would see me as I walked down the street couldn't help but compliment how good I look in my dress. I had a woman stop her car and honk and yell “You look so good! So good!” And they meant it. That felt so amazing. That was a recurring theme throughout the evening; women coming up and just saying, “Hey, you look beautiful” and that's something I'd never experienced either. As the day kept moving on, I noticed I would continue to have these amazingly close experiences with the marginalized in America. The quote-unquote “minorities” of this country. The blessed people who truly seek the Kingdom of Heaven. That hunger and thirst for rightiousness. There was a new kinship I'd never felt before because I was no longer presenting as the oppressor. I was no longer a cis, White, Male member of the patriarchy. I was an ally. To every woman, every person of color, to every member of the LGBTQ community. Even small short interactions. There's a different look in people's eyes that I had never gotten before because I was no longer presenting as the oppressor. It reminded me of something that happens several times in the Bible. In the Bible, when a Great King would draw the ire of God, whether through not obeying God's commands, by being greedy, by oppressing the poor, by not following God's Will, and by not living a life of righteousness and Justice; that person would be called to Humble themselves before God and clothe themselves in sackcloth and ashes. Sackcloth and Ashes is exactly what it sounds like. It's a burlap sack and the ashes from a long dead sacrificial fire to make yourself dirty. To make yourself low. Put on the Garb and the presentation of the lowliest of the low in your kingdom. To be a king and clothe yourself as the poorest of the poor and marginalized and walk among them and be treated as they. That's what it was to humble yourself before the Lord. And in doing that, the king would gain Humility, Knowledge, Wisdom and Understanding. Perspective of what it was like not to just be at the top, but to be at the bottom. I didn't realize it, but I was humbling myself. It was like seeing the world for the first time. Like having gone through my entire life without being able to see the color green, and realizing that a majority of the world was a color you didn't know existed. My eyes were opened. My heart was made even softer to what our Brothers and cisters go through every day in their struggle to just BE. Then it was time to use the restroom. A few years ago North Carolina had a big dust-up, like a lot of states did, about transgender bathrooms. They turned it into a big issue. They said “Men use the men's room. Women use the women's room.” It was shameful. It was petty. It was cruel. And now it was my turn. I could see the eyes following me as I was walking towards the restrooms. Just like everywhere else, there's a men's room and then right next to the men's room is the women's room. And I could see the eyes of the primarily white male audience wondering where I was going to go and I could see in their eyes them waiting for me to pick “The Wrong One.” There was no way I was going to go in the women's room. I don't identify as a transgendered person at all. It felt almost like they wanted me to. To have the excuse to start a fight. So, I went into the men's room as I planned. For anyone who's been to a baseball stadium like I was, you know that the men's bathrooms aren't exactly closed doors and high walls. It's an open line of urinals with no separators where you're essentially standing shoulder to shoulder with other men all holding their penicis. And I don't know why I didn't go to a stall. They were open and available. I don't know why, I just went to a urinal and used it. Let me be clear, I don't consider myself particularly courageous for any of this. It wasn't my intention. It was a learning experience. As I started to pee, I could feel the eyes on me. It was one of the most tense moments of my life, and I've been to War. I was as vulnerable as a person could be, member in hand in a Durham North Carolina Minor League Baseball bathroom, surrounded by men who had been hosted by Tito's Handmade Vodka for the last three hours. There I was. Waiting. Anticipating. Not sure. By the time I was done, and as I got out of that room, I let out a huge sigh of subconscious releif. More and more it just kept sinking into me, “My God! You are a confident, successful, strong 35 year old man who put on a sundress because you thought you would look good and it was comfortable. Imagine if you were an 18 year old trapped in the wrong body. Just trying to be comfortable. Just trying to live their own life. Imagine how absolutely terrified that person would be. They probably can't bench 300 pounds. They probably aren't a Marine Corps Martial Arts Instructor. If I can feel that fear, I can't even imagine. It makes my heart weep. As I walked back to my seat to rejoin the concert that was the first moment that I knew that today was a lot bigger than I had anticipated. And it was about to get a lot bigger. Unbeknownst to me at the time, this Friday just happened to be the Friday that the Supreme Court overturned Roe v-- Wade. So now there was this new added layer. An added layer both of camaraderie and of adversarial tension to say the least. Because now here I am a Man, Wearing a sundress. Presenting as a female (poorly at that.) In a room filled with women who had just had their rights taken away. And for the only time in my life I feel like an ally. I had always been one. I've gone to marches. I've stood next to my cisters as best I could. But now, Here, I was. It's hard to put the thoughts any emotion into that feeling. I felt sad like everyone else. But I felt in that moment, with my large masculine frame hugged in the delicate beauty of this traditionally feminine presenting sundress that I could actually be a source of strength and support for real. Not just in words, but in deeds and I I felt what I can only imagine women have been feeling their entire lives. And just a fraction of the pain that they must be feeling now. I could go on about the snide comments that were made throughout the day. They really didn't affect me after a few hours. You just kind of smile and laugh. I remember one older gentleman asked, “Hey, did you lose a bet?” and I just quickly responded “Nope, I am winning life!” and I really was. I had just had an opportunity to experience a portion of what so many people go through every day. I was blessed with a spiritual Insight, even for just a few hours, of to what I had though I had knew in my head, but never had to feel in my soul. I now see the value in humbling yourself truly.To take up the posture and the presentation of the downtrodden and marginalized so you can truly understand what it means, just a little bit better, what it means to love and support them. -Mathew 18:12-
item.dc:creator: ChrisGnight

item.title: I can not get this picture out of my head
item.description: Never been in a war. Have seen this before. My teacher used to "phase off" randomly and a friend who went to Afghanistan suddenly stopped talking in middle of conversation. They both had this look. They are both dead now. I cannot get this picture out of my head.
item.dc:creator: mittag

item.title: There's a line between hope and foolishness
item.description:
A South Florida Synagogue is suing the state over its 15-week abortion ban saying the procedure “is required if necessary to protect the health, mental or physical well-being” of a pregnant woman, according to Judaism. Rabbi Barry Silver says the overturning of Roe “criminalizes” his religion. He sat down with MSNBC host Katie Phang to explain. full segment: https://www.msnbc.com/katie-phang/watch/rabbi-barry-silver-the-overturning-of-roe-v-wade-is-an-abomination-142867013854
The Supreme Court's decisions this week tell a clear story about the right-wing majority's priorities, beyond just ripping away 50 years of protected rights for half of America's population. In fact, they've gone scorched earth, imposing their Christian theocratic agenda on all of us. Legal expert Elie Mystal joins Joy Reid with his analysis. full segment: https://www.msnbc.com/the-reidout/watch/supreme-court-is-imposing-christian-theocratic-agenda-on-america-joy-reid-says-142811205707
item.dc:creator: springelmogizmo

item.title: Old girl
item.description: Our old girl Skye during some rather wet weekend walkies. Thanks for the updates everyone! Skye is absolutely adored in our household.
item.dc:creator: ScottishSelkie
